source.title: Context and behavioral processes in extinction

confidence_rationale: Canonical review of context-dependent extinction and renewal effects. Source quality high (Bouton is the foundational researcher in this area; Learning & Memory is the canonical venue). Mapping fidelity moderate -- Bouton's contextual-renewal evidence is the closest behavioural analog to a per-region trust signal (the agent treats the same CS-US association as currently-trusted in one context and currently-untrusted in another), but the framework treats context as a categorical retrieval cue rather than as a continuous freshness scalar. Supports REE's premise that per-region/per-context model trust is a real biological phenomenon; weakens the strong-claim version that the literature already has the continuous-scalar machinery REE proposes.
failure_signatures: Bouton's contextual-renewal framework is categorical (context A vs context B retrieves different memories), not continuous (V_s scalar modulates trust on a graded scale).
The framework concerns acquisition/extinction of CS-US associations, not the broader REE notion of rule-arbitration weight modulation.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Extinction does not destroy the original CS-US association; it generates a new context-dependent association that inhibits the original. When the CS is tested in a context different from the extinction context (ABA renewal: trained in A, extinguished in B, tested in A; or ABC renewal: trained in A, extinguished in B, tested in novel context C), the original association reappears -- the extinguished response is 'renewed'. Context functions as a retrieval cue that selects which of multiple associations is currently in force. Extinction memories are particularly context-specific while acquisition memories generalise more broadly.
mapping.ree_translation: REE's V_s machinery (MECH-269/287) posits that the agent carries a per-region freshness signal that modulates trust in rules indexed in that region. Bouton's contextual-renewal framework demonstrates that biological agents do exhibit per-context memory selection: which CS-US association is in force depends on the current context, and switching contexts can effectively 'restore' a previously-suppressed association. This is the closest behavioural cousin to REE's per-region trust modulation. The fundamental phenomenon (context-dependent memory selection) is well-established. The structural difference is that Bouton's machinery is categorical and retrieval-based whereas REE's V_s is continuous and trust-modulation-based.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Two transfer risks. (1) Granularity -- categorical context cues vs continuous freshness scalars. REE's V_s could be interpreted as a continuous version of context, which has support in the model-of-context literature (Gershman/Niv state-uncertainty work) but goes beyond Bouton 2004. (2) Function -- Bouton's framework is about which memory is retrieved when responding to a CS; REE's V_s is about how much trust to place in a rule when arbitrating. These are not interchangeable.
mapping.source_context: Review paper synthesising Pavlovian and instrumental extinction studies in rats. Behavioural focus; some neural-substrate cross-references but not the primary content.

confidence_components.notes: Strong source quality. Mapping fidelity moderate: per-context memory-selection is well-established but the continuous-scalar form REE proposes goes beyond what this review documents.
